name: gwpy

metadata.name: gwpy
classifiers: License :: OSI Approved :: GNU General Public License v3 or later (GPLv3+)
metadata.summary: A python package for gravitational-wave astrophysics

documentation:
GWpy is a collaboration-driven Python package providing tools for
studying data from ground-based gravitational-wave detectors.

GWpy provides a user-friendly, intuitive interface to the common
time-domain and frequency-domain data produced by the LIGO and Virgo
observatories and their analyses, with easy-to-follow tutorials at each
step.

<https://gwpy.github.io>

# Release status

[![PyPI version](https://badge.fury.io/py/gwpy.svg)](http://badge.fury.io/py/gwpy)
[![Conda version](https://img.shields.io/conda/vn/conda-forge/gwpy.svg)](https://anaconda.org/conda-forge/gwpy/)

[![License](https://img.shields.io/pypi/l/gwpy.svg)](https://choosealicense.com/licenses/gpl-3.0/)
![Supported Python versions](https://img.shields.io/pypi/pyversions/gwpy.svg)

# Development status

[![Build status](https://gitlab.com/gwpy/gwpy/badges/main/pipeline.svg)](https://gitlab.com/gwpy/gwpy/-/pipelines/?ref=main)
[![Coverage status](https://gitlab.com/gwpy/gwpy/badges/main/coverage.svg)](https://gitlab.com/gwpy/gwpy/-/pipelines/?ref=main)

# Installation

To install, you can do:

```
conda install -c conda-forge gwpy
```

or

```
python -m pip install gwpy
```

You can test your installation, and its version by

```
python -c "import gwpy; print(gwpy.__version__)"
```

# License

GWpy is released under the GNU General Public License v3.0 or later, see
[here](https://choosealicense.com/licenses/gpl-3.0/) for a description of
this license, or see the
[LICENSE](https://gitlab.com/gwpy/gwpy/-/blob/main/LICENSE) file for the
full text.

functions.name: gwpy.io.utils.identify_factory
documentation:
Factory function to create I/O identifiers for a set of extensions

The returned function is designed for use in the unified I/O registry
via the `astropy.io.registry.register_identifier` hool.

Parameters
----------
extensions : `str`
    one or more file extension strings

Returns
-------
identifier : `callable`
    an identifier function that tests whether an incoming file path
    carries any of the given file extensions (using `str.endswith`)

functions.name: gwpy.io.utils.gopen
documentation:
Open a file handling optional gzipping

If `name` ends with `'.gz'`, or if the GZIP file signature is
found at the beginning of the file, the file will be opened with
`gzip.open`, otherwise a regular file will be returned from `open`.

# Parameters

name

: path or name of file to open.

args, kwargs

: other arguments to pass to either `open` for regular files, or
  `gzip.open` for gzipped files.

# Returns

file

: the open file object

functions.name: gwpy.io.utils.with_open
documentation:
Decorate a function to ensure the chosen argument is an open file

# Parameters

func

: the function to decorate

mode

: the mode with which to open files

pos

: which argument to look at

# Examples

To ensure that the first argument is an open read-only file, just use
the decorator without functional parentheses or arguments:

```pycon
>>> @with_open
>>> def my_func(pathorfile, *args, **kwargs)
>>>     ...
```

To ensure that the second argument (position 1) is a file open for writing:

```pycon
>>> @with_open(mode="w", pos=1)
>>> def my_func(stuff, pathorfile, *args, **kwargs)
>>>     stuff.write_to(pathorfile, *args, **kwargs)
```

functions.name: gwpy.io.utils.file_list
documentation:
Parse a number of possible input types into a list of filepaths.

# Parameters

flist

: the input data container, normally just a single file path, or a list
  of paths, but can generally be any of the following

  - `str` representing a single file path (or comma-separated collection)
  - open `file` or `~gzip.GzipFile` object
  - {class}`~lal.utils.CacheEntry`
  - `str` with `.cache` or `.lcf` extension
  - simple `list` or `tuple` of `str` paths

# Returns

files

: `list` of `str` file paths

# Raises

ValueError

: if the input `flist` cannot be interpreted as any of the above inputs

functions.name: gwpy.io.utils.file_path
documentation:
Determine the path of a file.

This doesn't do any sanity checking to check that the file
actually exists, or is readable.

# Parameters

fobj

: the file object or path to parse

# Returns

path

: the path of the underlying file

# Raises

ValueError

: if a file path cannnot be determined

# Examples

```pycon
>>> from gwpy.io.utils import file_path
>>> import pathlib
>>> file_path("test.txt")
'test.txt'
>>> file_path(pathlib.Path('dir') / 'test.txt')
'dir/test.txt'
>>> file_path(open("test.txt", "r"))
'test.txt'
>>> file_path("file:///home/user/test.txt")
'/home/user/test.txt'
```

modules.name: gwpy.io.datafind
documentation:
Utilities for auto-discovery of GW data files.

Automatic discovery of file paths for both LIGO and Virgo index solutions
(`gwdatafind` or `ffl`, respectvely) is supported.

The functions in this module are highly reliant on having local access to
files (either directly, or via NFS, or CVMFS).

Data discovery using the DataFind service requires the `gwdatafind` Python
package (a dependency of `gwpy`), and either the `GW_DATAFIND_SERVER`
(or legacy `LIGO_DATAFIND_SERVER`) environment variable to be set,
or the `host` keyword must be passed to {func}`find_urls` and friends.

Data discovery using the Virgo FFL system requires the `FFLPATH` environment
variable to point to the directory containing FFL files, **or** the
`VIRGODATA` environment variable to point to a directory containing an
ffl\` subdirectory, which contains FFL files.
-*- coding: utf-8 -*-

functions.name: gwpy.io.datafind._type_priority
documentation:
Prioritise the given GWF type based on its name or trend status.

This is essentially an ad-hoc ordering function based on internal knowledge
of how LIGO does GWF type naming.

functions.name: gwpy.io.datafind.on_tape
documentation:
Determine whether any of the given files are on tape

Parameters
----------
*files : `str`
    one or more paths to GWF files

Returns
-------
True/False : `bool`
    `True` if any of the files are determined to be on tape,
    otherwise `False`

functions.name: gwpy.io.datafind._gwdatafind_module
documentation:
Return the appropriate GWDataFind-like API based on the environment

This allows switching to the hacky `gwpy.io.ffldatafind` replacement
module to enable a GWDataFind-like interface for direct FFL data
discovery at Virgo.

functions.name: gwpy.io.datafind._select_gwdatafind_mod
documentation:
Decorate a function to see the right `gwdatafind` API.

This exists only to allow on-the-fly replacing of the actual `gwdatafind`
with {mod}`gwpy.io.ffldatafind` if it looks like we are trying to find
data from FFL files.

Discover gaps in a datafind/ffl archive for the given ifo/type

functions.name: gwpy.io.datafind._rank_types
documentation:
Rank and sort the matched frametypes according to some criteria

`matches` is a dict of (channel, [(type, gwf, gapsize), ...])

functions.name: gwpy.io.datafind.find_frametype
documentation:
Find the frametype(s) that hold data for a given channel

# Parameters

channel

: the channel to be found

gpstime

: target GPS time at which to find correct type

frametype_match

: regular expression to use for frametype `str` matching

host

: name of datafind host to use

port

: port on datafind host to use

return_all

: return all found types, default is to return to 'best' match

allow_tape

: do not test types whose frame files are stored on tape (not on
  spinning disk)

on_gaps

: action to take when gaps are discovered in datafind coverage,
  default: `'error'`, i.e. don't match frametypes with gaps.
  Select `'ignore'` to ignore gaps, or `'warn'` to display
  warnings when gaps are found in a datafind `find_urls` query

# Returns

If a single name is given, and `return_all=False` (default):

frametype

: name of best match frame type

If a single name is given, and `return_all=True`:

types

: the list of all matching frame types

If multiple names are given, the above return types are wrapped into a
`dict` of `(channel, type_or_list)` pairs.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> from gwpy.io import datafind as io_datafind
>>> io_datafind.find_frametype('H1:IMC-PWR_IN_OUTPUT', gpstime=1126259462)
'H1_R'
>>> io_datafind.find_frametype('H1:IMC-PWR_IN_OUTPUT', gpstime=1126259462,
...                            return_all=True)
['H1_R', 'H1_C']
>>> io_datafind.find_frametype(
...     ('H1:IMC-PWR_IN_OUTPUT', 'H1:OMC-DCPD_SUM_OUTPUT',
...      'H1:GDS-CALIB_STRAIN'),
...     gpstime=1126259462, return_all=True))"
{'H1:GDS-CALIB_STRAIN': ['H1_HOFT_C00'],
 'H1:OMC-DCPD_SUM_OUTPUT': ['H1_R', 'H1_C'],
 'H1:IMC-PWR_IN_OUTPUT': ['H1_R', 'H1_C']}
```

functions.name: gwpy.io.datafind.find_best_frametype
documentation:
Intelligently select the best frametype from which to read this channel

# Parameters

channel

: the channel to be found

start

: GPS start time of period of interest,
  any input parseable by `~gwpy.time.to_gps` is fine

end

: GPS end time of period of interest,
  any input parseable by `~gwpy.time.to_gps` is fine

host

: name of datafind host to use

port

: port on datafind host to use

frametype_match

: regular expression to use for frametype `str` matching

allow_tape

: do not test types whose frame files are stored on tape (not on
  spinning disk)

# Returns

frametype

: the best matching frametype for the `channel` in the
  `[start, end)` interval

# Raises

ValueError

: if no valid frametypes are found

# Examples

```pycon
>>> from gwpy.io.datafind import find_best_frametype
>>> find_best_frametype('L1:GDS-CALIB_STRAIN', 1126259460, 1126259464)
'L1_HOFT_C00'
```

documentation:
Returns the NDS2-formatted name for a channel

Understands how to format NDS name strings from
`gwpy.detector.Channel` and `nds2.channel` objects

documentation: Maps `_get_nds2_name` for a list of input channels

functions.name: gwpy.io.nds2.parse_nds_env
documentation:
Parse the NDSSERVER environment variable into a list of hosts

# Parameters

env

: environment variable name to use for server order,
  default `'NDSSERVER'`. The contents of this variable should
  be a comma-separated list of `host:port` strings, e.g.
  `'nds1.server.com:80,nds2.server.com:80'`

# Returns

hostiter

: a list of (unique) `(str, int)` tuples for each host:port
  pair

functions.name: gwpy.io.nds2.host_resolution_order
documentation:
Generate a logical ordering of NDS (host, port) tuples for this IFO

# Parameters

ifo

: prefix for IFO of interest

env

: environment variable name to use for server order,
  default `'NDSSERVER'`. The contents of this variable should
  be a comma-separated list of `host:port` strings, e.g.
  `'nds1.server.com:80,nds2.server.com:80'`

epoch

: GPS epoch of data requested

lookback

: duration of spinning-disk cache. This value triggers defaulting to
  the CIT NDS2 server over those at the LIGO sites

# Returns

hro

: ordered `list` of `(host, port)` tuples

functions.name: gwpy.io.nds2.connect
documentation:
Open an `nds2.connection` to a given host and port

Parameters
----------
host : `str`
    name of server with which to connect

port : `int`, optional
    connection port

Returns
-------
connection : `nds2.connection`
    a new open connection to the given NDS host

functions.name: gwpy.io.nds2.auth_connect
documentation:
Open an `nds2.connection` handling simple authentication errors

This method will catch exceptions related to kerberos authentication,
and execute a kinit() for the user before attempting to connect again.

Parameters
----------
host : `str`
    name of server with which to connect

port : `int`, optional
    connection port

Returns
-------
connection : `nds2.connection`
    a new open connection to the given NDS host

functions.name: gwpy.io.nds2.open_connection
documentation: Decorate a function to create a `nds2.connection` if required

functions.name: gwpy.io.nds2.parse_nds2_enums
documentation: Decorate a function to translate a type string into an integer

functions.name: gwpy.io.nds2.reset_epoch
documentation:
Wrap a function to reset the epoch when finished

This is useful for functions that wish to use `connection.set_epoch`.

functions.name: gwpy.io.nds2.find_channels
documentation:
Query an NDS2 server for channel information

# Parameters

channels

: list of channel names to query, each can include bash-style globs

connection

: open NDS2 connection to use for query

host

: name of NDS2 server to query, required if `connection` is not
  given

port

: port number on host to use for NDS2 connection

sample_rate

: a single number, representing a specific sample rate to match,
  or a tuple representing a (low, high)\` interval to match

type

: the NDS2 channel type to match

dtype

: the NDS2 data type to match

unique

: require one (and only one) match per channel

epoch

: the NDS epoch to restrict to, either the name of a known epoch,
  or a 2-tuple of GPS `[start, stop)` times

# Returns

channels

: list of NDS2 channel objects

# See also

nds2.connection.find_channels

: for documentation on the underlying query method

# Examples

```pycon
>>> from gwpy.io.nds2 import find_channels
>>> find_channels(['G1:DER_DATA_H'], host='nds.ligo.caltech.edu')
[<G1:DER_DATA_H (16384Hz, RDS, FLOAT64)>]
```

functions.name: gwpy.io.nds2._find_channel
documentation:
Internal method to find a single channel

Parameters
----------
connection : `nds2.connection`, optional
    open NDS2 connection to use for query

name : `str`
    the name of the channel to find

ctype : `int`
    the NDS2 channel type to match

dtype : `int`
    the NDS2 data type to match

sample_rate : `tuple`
    a pre-formatted rate tuple (see `find_channels`)

unique : `bool`, optional, default: `False`
    require one (and only one) match per channel

Returns
-------
channels : `list` of `nds2.channel`
    list of NDS2 channel objects, if `unique=True` is given the list
    is guaranteed to have only one element.

See also
--------
nds2.connection.find_channels
    for documentation on the underlying query method

functions.name: gwpy.io.nds2._strip_ctype
documentation:
Strip the ctype from a channel name for the given nds server version

This is needed because NDS1 servers store trend channels _including_
the suffix, but not raw channels, and NDS2 doesn't do this.

functions.name: gwpy.io.nds2.get_availability
documentation:
Query an NDS2 server for data availability

# Parameters

channels

: list of channel names to query; this list is mapped to NDS channel
  names using {func}`find_channels`.

start

: GPS start time of query

end

: GPS end time of query

connection

: open NDS2 connection to use for query

host

: name of NDS2 server to query, required if `connection` is not
  given

port

: port number on host to use for NDS2 connection

# Returns

segdict

: dict of `(name, SegmentList)` pairs

# Raises

ValueError

: if the given channel name cannot be mapped uniquely to a name
  in the NDS server database.

# See also

nds2.connection.get_availability

: for documentation on the underlying query method

functions.name: gwpy.io.nds2.minute_trend_times
documentation:
Expand a \[start, end) interval for use in querying for minute trends

NDS2 requires start and end times for minute trends to be a multiple of
60 (to exactly match the time of a minute-trend sample), so this function
expands the given `[start, end)` interval to the nearest multiples.

# Parameters

start

: GPS start time of query

end

: GPS end time of query

# Returns

mstart

: `start` rounded down to nearest multiple of 60

mend

: `end` rounded up to nearest multiple of 60

functions.name: gwpy.io.gwf.identify_gwf
documentation:
Identify a filename or file object as GWF

This function is overloaded in that it will also identify a cache file
as 'gwf' if the first entry in the cache contains a GWF file extension

functions.name: gwpy.io.gwf.open_gwf
documentation:
Open a filename for reading or writing GWF format data

# Parameters

filename

: the path to read from, or write to

mode

: either `'r'` (read) or `'w'` (write)

# Returns

`LDAStools.frameCPP.IFrameFStream`

: the input frame stream (if `mode='r'`), or

`LDAStools.frameCPP.IFrameFStream`

: the output frame stream (if `mode='w'`)

functions.name: gwpy.io.gwf.write_frames
documentation:
Write a list of frame objects to a file

**Requires:** [LDAStools.frameCPP]

# Parameters

filename

: path to write into

frames

: list of frames to write into file

compression

: name of compresion algorithm to use, or its endian-appropriate
  ID, choose from

  - `'RAW'`
  - `'GZIP'`
  - `'DIFF_GZIP'`
  - `'ZERO_SUPPRESS'`
  - `'ZERO_SUPPRESS_OTHERWISE_GZIP'`

compression_level

: compression level for given method, default is `6` for GZIP-based
  methods, otherwise `0`

functions.name: gwpy.io.gwf.create_frame
documentation:
Create a new {class}`~LDAStools.frameCPP.FrameH`

**Requires:** [LDAStools.frameCPP]

# Parameters

time

: frame start time in GPS seconds

duration

: frame length in seconds

name

: name of project or other experiment description

run

: run number (number < 0 reserved for simulated data); monotonic for
  experimental runs

ifos

: list of interferometer prefices (e.g. `'L1'`) associated with this
  frame

# Returns

frame

: the newly created frame header

functions.name: gwpy.io.gwf.create_fradcdata
documentation:
Create a `~frameCPP.FrAdcData` from a `~gwpy.types.Series`

:::{note}
Currently this method is restricted to 1-dimensional arrays.
:::

# Parameters

series

: the input data array to store

frame_epoch

: the GPS start epoch of the `Frame` that will contain this
  data structure

# Returns

frdata

: the newly created data structure

# Notes

See Table 10 (§4.3.2.4) of LIGO-T970130 for more details

functions.name: gwpy.io.gwf.create_frprocdata
documentation:
Create a `~frameCPP.FrAdcData` from a `~gwpy.types.Series`

:::{note}
Currently this method is restricted to 1-dimensional arrays.
:::

# Parameters

series

: the input data array to store

frame_epoch

: the GPS start epoch of the `Frame` that will contain this
  data structure

comment

: comment

type

: type of data object

subtype

: subtype for f-Series

trange

: duration of sampled data

fshift

: frequency in the original data that corresponds to 0 Hz in the
  heterodyned series

phase

: phase of the heterodyning signal at start of dataset

frange

: frequency range

bandwidth

: reoslution bandwidth

# Returns

frdata

: the newly created data structure

# Notes

See Table 17 (§4.3.2.11) of LIGO-T970130 for more details

functions.name: gwpy.io.gwf.create_frsimdata
documentation:
Create a `~frameCPP.FrAdcData` from a `~gwpy.types.Series`

:::{note}
Currently this method is restricted to 1-dimensional arrays.
:::

# Parameters

series

: the input data array to store

frame_epoch

: the GPS start epoch of the `Frame` that will contain this
  data structure

fshift

: frequency in the original data that corresponds to 0 Hz in the
  heterodyned series

phase

: phase of the heterodyning signal at start of dataset

# Returns

frdata

: the newly created data structure

# Notes

See Table 20 (§4.3.2.14) of LIGO-T970130 for more details

functions.name: gwpy.io.gwf.create_frvect
documentation:
Create a `~frameCPP.FrVect` from a `~gwpy.types.Series`

:::{note}
Currently this method is restricted to 1-dimensional arrays.
:::

# Parameters

series

: the input data array to store

# Returns

frvect

: the newly created data vector

functions.name: gwpy.io.gwf.num_channels
documentation:
Find the total number of channels in this framefile

**Requires:** |LDAStools.frameCPP|_

Parameters
----------
framefile : `str`
    path to GWF-format file on disk

Returns
-------
n : `int`
    the total number of channels found in the table of contents for this
    file

functions.name: gwpy.io.gwf.get_channel_type
documentation:
Find the channel type in a given GWF file

**Requires:** |LDAStools.frameCPP|_

Parameters
----------
channel : `str`, `~gwpy.detector.Channel`
    name of data channel to find

framefile : `str`
    path of GWF file in which to search

Returns
-------
ctype : `str`
    the type of the channel ('adc', 'sim', or 'proc')

Raises
------
ValueError
    if the channel is not found in the table-of-contents

functions.name: gwpy.io.gwf.channel_in_frame
documentation:
Determine whether a channel is stored in this framefile

**Requires:** |LDAStools.frameCPP|_

Parameters
----------
channel : `str`
    name of channel to find

framefile : `str`
    path of GWF file to test

Returns
-------
inframe : `bool`
    whether this channel is included in the table of contents for
    the given framefile

functions.name: gwpy.io.gwf.iter_channel_names
documentation:
Iterate over the names of channels found in a GWF file

**Requires:** |LDAStools.frameCPP|_

Parameters
----------
framefile : `str`
    path of GWF file to read

Returns
-------
channels : `generator`
    an iterator that will loop over the names of channels as read from
    the table of contents of the given GWF file

functions.name: gwpy.io.gwf.get_channel_names
documentation:
Return a list of all channel names found in a GWF file

This method just returns

```pycon
>>> list(iter_channel_names(framefile))
```

**Requires:** [LDAStools.frameCPP]

# Parameters

framefile

: path of GWF file to read

# Returns

channels

: a `list` of channel names as read from the table of contents of
  the given GWF file

functions.name: gwpy.io.gwf._iter_channels
documentation:
Yields the name and type of each channel in a GWF file TOC

**Requires:** |LDAStools.frameCPP|_

Parameters
----------
framefile : `str`, `LDAStools.frameCPP.IFrameFStream`
    path of GWF file, or open file stream, to read

functions.name: gwpy.io.gwf.data_segments
documentation:
Returns the segments containing data for a channel

**Requires:** |LDAStools.frameCPP|_

A frame is considered to contain data if a valid FrData structure
(of any type) exists for the channel in that frame.  No checks
are directly made against the underlying FrVect structures.

Parameters
----------
paths : `list` of `str`
    a list of GWF file paths

channel : `str`
    the name to check in each frame

warn : `bool`, optional
    emit a `UserWarning` when a channel is not found in a frame

Returns
-------
segments : `~gwpy.segments.SegmentList`
    the list of segments containing data

functions.name: gwpy.io.gwf._gwf_channel_segments
documentation: Yields the segments containing data for `channel` in this GWF path

functions.name: gwpy.io.gwf._get_type
documentation:
Handle a type string, or just return an `int`

Only to be called in relation to FrProcDataType and FrProcDataSubType

functions.name: gwpy.io.gwf._get_frprocdata_type

functions.name: gwpy.io.gwf._get_frprocdata_subtype

functions.name: gwpy.io.gwf._series_name
documentation:
Returns the 'name' of a `Series` that should be written to GWF

This is basically `series.name or str(series.channel) or ""`

Parameters
----------
series : `gwpy.types.Series`
    the input series that will be written

Returns
-------
name : `str`
    the name to use when storing this series

functions.name: gwpy.io.mp.read_multi
documentation:
Read sources into a `cls` with multiprocessing

This method should be called by `cls.read` and uses the `nproc`
keyword to enable and handle pool-based multiprocessing of
multiple source files, using `flatten` to combine the
chunked data into a single object of the correct type.

# Parameters

flatten

: a method to take a list of `cls` instances, and combine them
  into a single `cls` instance

cls

: the object type to read

source

: the input data source, can be of in many different forms

args

: positional arguments to pass to the reader

kwargs

: keyword arguments to pass to the reader

functions.name: gwpy.io.mp._read_single_file
documentation:
Reads a single file and returns the output

This is designed only to be passed to
{func}`gwpy.utils.mp.multiprocess_with_queues`

# Returns

f, output :

: the input file (object) that was (attempted to be) read, and
  the result of the read operation, which may be an exception
  object.

functions.name: gwpy.io.hdf5.open_hdf5
documentation:
Wrapper to open a {class}`h5py.File` from disk, gracefully
handling a few corner cases

functions.name: gwpy.io.hdf5.with_read_hdf5
documentation:
Decorate an HDF5-reading function to open a filepath if needed

`func` should be written to presume an `h5py.Group` as the first
positional argument.

functions.name: gwpy.io.hdf5.find_dataset
documentation:
Find and return the relevant dataset inside the given H5 object

If `path=None` is given, and `h5o` contains a single dataset, that
will be returned

# Parameters

h5o

: the HDF5 object in which to search

path

: the path (relative to `h5o`) of the desired data set

# Returns

dset

: the recovered dataset object

# Raises

ValueError

: if `path=None` and the HDF5 object contains multiple datasets

KeyError

: if `path` is given but is not found within the HDF5 object

functions.name: gwpy.io.hdf5.with_write_hdf5
documentation:
Decorate an HDF5-writing function to open a filepath if needed

`func` should be written to take the object to be written as the
first argument, and then presume an `h5py.Group` as the second.

This method uses keywords `append` and `overwrite` as follows if
the output file already exists:

- `append=False, overwrite=False`: raise `~exceptions.IOError`
- `append=True`: open in mode `a`
- `append=False, overwrite=True`: open in mode `w`

functions.name: gwpy.io.hdf5.create_dataset
documentation:
Create a new dataset inside the parent HDF5 object

# Parameters

parent

: the object in which to create a new dataset

path

: the path at which to create the new dataset

overwrite

: if `True`, delete any existing dataset at the desired path,
  default: `False`

kwargs

: other arguments are passed directly to
  {meth}`h5py.Group.create_dataset`

# Returns

dataset

: the newly created dataset

functions.name: gwpy.io.ffldatafind._handle_error
documentation:
Handle error, warn, or ignore for the given state.

# Parameters

action

: The action to perform, one of
  `'warn'` (emit a `UserWarning`, default),
  `'ignore'` (do nothing),
  or anything else (raise a `RuntimeError`).

message

: The message to emit with warnings or errors.

# Raises

RuntimeError

: If action is not `'warn'` or `'ignore'`.

functions.name: gwpy.io.ffldatafind.find_types
documentation:
Return the list of known data types.

Parameters
----------
site : `str`, optional
    Observatory ID (e.g. ``'A'`)) to retrict types, if `None` (default)
    is given, all types are returned.

match : `str`, optional
    Regular expression to use to restrict types.

Returns
-------
types : `list` of `str`
    The list of data types matching the criteria.

functions.name: gwpy.io.ffldatafind.find_urls
documentation:
Return the list of all files of the given type in the \[start, end)
GPS interval.

# Parameters

site

: Observatory ID to search for.

tag

: Data type tag to search for.

gpsstart

: GPS start time of query.

gpsend

: GPS end time of query.

match

: Regular expression to use to retrict returned data URLs.

on_gaps

: What to do if the full GPS interval is not covered, one of
  `'warn'` (emit a `UserWarning`, default),
  `'ignore'` (do nothing),
  anything else (raise a `RuntimeError`).

# Returns

urls

: A list of URLs representing discovered data.

functions.name: gwpy.io.ffldatafind.find_latest
documentation:
Return the most recent file of a given type.

# Parameters

site

: Observatory ID to search for.

tag

: Data type tag to search for.

on_missing

: What to do if a URL is not found for the given site and tag, one of
  `'warn'` (emit a `UserWarning`, default),
  `'ignore'` (do nothing),
  anything else (raise a `RuntimeError`).

# Returns

urls

: A list (typically of one item) of URLs representing the latest data
  for a specific site and tag.

modules.name: gwpy.io.kerberos
documentation:
Utility module to initialise a kerberos ticket for NDS2 connections

This module provides a lazy-mans python version of the 'kinit'
command-line tool, with internal guesswork using keytabs

See the documentation of the `kinit` function for example usage
-*- coding: utf-8 -*-
Copyright (C) Duncan Macleod (2014-2020)
This file is part of GWpy.
GWpy is free software: you can redistribute it and/or modify
it under the terms of the GNU General Public License as published by
the Free Software Foundation, either version 3 of the License, or
(at your option) any later version.
GWpy is distributed in the hope that it will be useful,
but WITHOUT ANY WARRANTY; without even the implied warranty of
MERCHANTABILITY or FITNESS FOR A PARTICULAR PURPOSE.  See the
GNU General Public License for more details.
You should have received a copy of the GNU General Public License
along with GWpy.  If not, see <http://www.gnu.org/licenses/>.

functions.name: gwpy.io.kerberos.kinit
documentation:
Initialise a kerberos ticket using the `kinit` command-line tool.

This allows authenticated connections to, amongst others, NDS2 services.

# Parameters

username

: name of user, will be prompted for if not given.

password

: cleartext password of user for given realm, will be prompted for
  if not given.

realm

: name of realm to authenticate against, read from keytab if available,
  defaults to `'LIGO.ORG'`.

exe

: path to kinit executable.

keytab

: path to keytab file. If not given this will be read from the
  `KRB5_KTNAME` environment variable. See notes for more details.

krb5ccname

: path to Kerberos credentials cache.

verbose

: print verbose output (if `True`), or not (`False)`; default is `True`
  if any user-prompting is needed, otherwise `False`.

# Notes

If a keytab is given, or is read from the `KRB5_KTNAME` environment
variable, this will be used to guess the username and realm, if it
contains only a single credential.

# Examples

Example 1: standard user input, with password prompt:

```
>>> kinit('albert.einstein')
```

Password for <mailto:albert.einstein@LIGO.ORG>:
Kerberos ticket generated for <mailto:albert.einstein@LIGO.ORG>

Example 2: extract username and realm from keytab, and use that
in authentication:

```
>>> kinit(keytab='~/.kerberos/ligo.org.keytab', verbose=True)
```

Kerberos ticket generated for <mailto:albert.einstein@LIGO.ORG>

functions.name: gwpy.io.kerberos.parse_keytab
documentation:
Read the contents of a KRB5 keytab file, returning a list of
credentials listed within

# Parameters

keytab

: path to keytab file

# Returns

creds

: the (unique) list of `(username, realm, kvno)` as read from the
  keytab file

# Examples

```pycon
>>> from gwpy.io.kerberos import parse_keytab
>>> print(parse_keytab("creds.keytab"))
[('albert.einstein', 'LIGO.ORG', 1)]
```

Build a `PartialLIGOLWContentHandler` to read only this element

functions.name: gwpy.io.ligolw.read_ligolw
documentation:
Read one or more LIGO_LW format files

# Parameters

source

contenthandler

# Returns

xmldoc

functions.name: gwpy.io.ligolw.read_table
documentation:
Read a {class}`~ligo.lw.table.Table` from one or more LIGO_LW files

# Parameters

source

: object representing one or more files. One of

  - a LIGO_LW {class}`~ligo.lw.ligolw.Document`
  - an open `file`
  - a `str` pointing to a file path on disk
  - a formatted {class}`~lal.utils.CacheEntry` representing one file
  - a `list` of `str` file paths or {class}`~lal.utils.CacheEntry`

tablename

: name of the table to read.

columns

: list of column name strings to read, default all.

contenthandler

: SAX content handler for parsing LIGO_LW documents.

kwargs

: other keyword arguments are passed to `~gwpy.io.ligolw.read_ligolw`

# Returns

table

: `Table` of data

functions.name: gwpy.io.ligolw.open_xmldoc
documentation:
Try and open an existing LIGO_LW-format file, or create a new Document

# Parameters

fobj

: file path or open file object to read

contenthandler

: the content handler with which to parse the document, if not given
  a default handler will be created using
  {func}`default_content_handler`.

kwargs

: other keyword arguments to pass to
  {func}`~ligo.lw.utils.load_fileobj` as appropriate

# Returns

xmldoc

: either the `Document` as parsed from an existing file, or a new, empty

overwrite

functions.name: gwpy.io.ligolw.write_tables
documentation:
Write an LIGO_LW table to file

# Parameters

target

: the file or document to write into

tables

: the tables to write

append

: if `True`, append to an existing file/table, otherwise `overwrite`

overwrite

: if `True`, delete an existing instance of the table type, otherwise
  append new rows

contenthandler

: the content handler with which to parse the document, if not given
  a default handler will be created using
  {func}`default_content_handler`.

kwargs

: other keyword arguments to pass to
  {func}`~ligo.lw.utils.load_fileobj` as appropriate

functions.name: gwpy.io.ligolw.iter_tables
documentation:
Iterate over all tables in the given document(s)

# Parameters

source

: one or more open files, file paths, or LIGO_LW Document\`s

# Yields

ligo.lw.table.Table

: a table structure from the document(s)

functions.name: gwpy.io.ligolw.list_tables
documentation:
List the names of all tables in this file(s)

# Parameters

source

: one or more open files, file paths, or LIGO_LW Document\`s

# Examples

```pycon
>>> from gwpy.io.ligolw import list_tables
>>> print(list_tables('H1-LDAS_STRAIN-968654552-10.xml.gz'))
['process', 'process_params', 'sngl_burst', 'search_summary', 'segment_definer', 'segment_summary', 'segment']
```

functions.name: gwpy.io.ligolw.to_table_type
documentation:
Cast a value to the correct type for inclusion in a LIGO_LW table

This method returns the input unmodified if a type mapping for `colname`
isn't found.

# Parameters

val

: The input object to convert, of any type

cls

: the table class to map against

colname

: The name of the mapping column

# Returns

obj

: The input `val` cast to the correct type

# Examples

```pycon
>>> from gwpy.io.ligolw import to_table_type as to_ligolw_type
>>> from ligo.lw.lsctables import SnglBurstTable
>>> x = to_ligolw_type(1.0, SnglBurstTable, 'central_freq'))
>>> print(type(x), x)
<class 'numpy.float32'> 1.0
```

documentation: Temporarily silence debug warnings from LAL.

functions.name: gwpy.io.cache._iter_cache
documentation:
Internal method that yields a `_CacheEntry` for each line in the file

This method supports reading LAL- and (nested) FFL-format cache files.

functions.name: gwpy.io.cache.read_cache
documentation:
Read a LAL- or FFL-format cache file as a list of file paths

Parameters
----------
cachefile : `str`, `pathlib.Path`, `file`
    Input file or file path to read.

coltype : `LIGOTimeGPS`, `int`, optional
    Type for GPS times.

sort : `callable`, optional
    A callable key function by which to sort the output list of file paths

segment : `gwpy.segments.Segment`, optional
    A GPS `[start, stop)` interval, if given only files overlapping this
    interval will be returned.

strict : `bool`, optional
    If `False` warn about entries that don't follow the LIGO-T050017
    standard, then skip them; if `True` all errors are raised as
    exceptions.

Returns
-------
paths : `list` of `str`
    A list of file paths as read from the cache file.

functions.name: gwpy.io.cache.read_cache_entry
documentation:
Read a file path from a line in a cache file.

Parameters
----------
line : `str`, `bytes`

gpstype : `LIGOTimeGPS`, `int`, optional

------
ValueError
    if the line cannot be parsed successfully

functions.name: gwpy.io.cache.write_cache

Write a `list` of cache entries to a file

# Parameters

fobj

format

functions.name: gwpy.io.cache.is_cache_entry
documentation:
Returns `True` if `path` can be represented as a cache entry

In practice this just tests whether the input is [LIGO-T050017] compliant.

# Parameters

path

: The input to test

# Returns

isentry

: `True` if `path` is an instance of `CacheEntry`, or can be parsed
  using [LIGO-T050017].

functions.name: gwpy.io.cache.filename_metadata
documentation:
Return metadata parsed from a filename following LIGO-T050017

This method is lenient with regards to integers in the GPS start time of
the file, as opposed to `gwdatafind.utils.filename_metadata`, which is
strict.

# Parameters

filename

: the path name of a file

# Returns

obs

: the observatory metadata

tag

: the file tag

segment

: the GPS `[float, float)` interval for this file

# Notes

[LIGO-T050017](https://dcc.ligo.org/LIGO-T050017) declares a
file naming convention that includes documenting the GPS start integer
and integer duration of a file, see that document for more details.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> from gwpy.io.cache import filename_metadata
>>> filename_metadata("A-B-0-1.txt")
('A', 'B', Segment(0, 1))
>>> filename_metadata("A-B-0.456-1.345.txt")
("A", "B", Segment(0.456, 1.801))
```

functions.name: gwpy.io.cache.file_segment
documentation:
Return the data segment for a filename following T050017

# Parameters

filename

: the path name of a file

# Returns

segment

: the `[start, stop)` GPS segment covered by the given file

# Notes

[LIGO-T050017] declares a filenaming convention that includes
documenting the GPS start integer and integer duration of a file,
see that document for more details.

functions.name: gwpy.io.cache.cache_segments
documentation:
Returns the segments of data covered by entries in the cache(s).

# Parameters

caches

: One or more lists of file paths
  (`str` or {class}`~lal.utils.CacheEntry`).

# Returns

segments

: A list of segments for when data should be available

functions.name: gwpy.io.cache.flatten
documentation:
Flatten a nested list of cache entries

# Parameters

caches

  (`str` or {class}`~lal.utils.CacheEntry`).

# Returns

flat

  each input.

functions.name: gwpy.io.cache.find_contiguous
documentation:
Separate one or more cache entry lists into time-contiguous sub-lists

# Parameters

caches

: One or more lists of file paths
  (`str` or {class}`~lal.utils.CacheEntry`).

# Returns

caches

: an interable yielding each contiguous cache

functions.name: gwpy.io.cache.sieve
documentation:
Filter the cache to find those entries that overlap `segment`

# Parameters

cache

: Input list of file paths

segment

: The `[start, stop)` interval to match against.

strict

: If `True` raise all exceptions, if `False` emit warnings when
  the file segment cannot be determined.

# Warns

UserWarning

: For any files for which the file segment cannot be determined,
  if `strict=False` is given; these files are excluded from the
  sieved cache.

array

: a file path to write to, or an `h5py.Group` in which to create

: the path inside the group at which to create the new dataset,
  defaults to `array.name`

attrs

: extra metadata to write into `h5py.Dataset.attrs`, on top of
  the default metadata

append

: if `True`, write new dataset to existing file, otherwise an

overwrite

  otherwise an exception will be raised if a dataset exists with

: the newly created dataset

methods.name: gwpy.types.array2d.Array2D._wrap_function
documentation: all of these try to return Quantities rather than simple numbers

A two-dimensional array with metadata

Parameters
----------
value : array-like
    input data array

unit : `~astropy.units.Unit`, optional
    physical unit of these data

x0 : `float`, `~astropy.units.Quantity`, optional, default: `0`
    the starting value for the x-axis of this array

dx : `float`, `~astropy.units.Quantity, optional, default: `1`
    the step size for the x-axis of this array

xindex : `array-like`
    the complete array of x-axis values for this array. This argument
    takes precedence over `x0` and `dx` so should be
    given in place of these if relevant, not alongside

xunit : `~astropy.units.Unit`, optional
    the unit of the x-axis coordinates. If not given explicitly, it will be
    taken from any of `dx`, `x0`, or `xindex`, or set to a boring default

y0 : `float`, `~astropy.units.Quantity`, optional, default: `0`
    the starting value for the y-axis of this array

dy : `float`, `~astropy.units.Quantity, optional, default: `1`
    the step size for the y-axis of this array

yindex : `array-like`
    the complete array of y-axis values for this array. This argument
    takes precedence over `y0` and `dy` so should be
    given in place of these if relevant, not alongside

yunit : `~astropy.units.Unit`, optional
    the unit of the y-axis coordinates. If not given explicitly, it will be
    taken from any of `dy`, `y0`, or `yindex`, or set to a boring default

epoch : `~gwpy.time.LIGOTimeGPS`, `float`, `str`, optional
    GPS epoch associated with these data,
    any input parsable by `~gwpy.time.to_gps` is fine

name : `str`, optional
    descriptive title for this array

channel : `~gwpy.detector.Channel`, `str`, optional
    source data stream for these data

dtype : `~numpy.dtype`, optional
    input data type

copy : `bool`, optional, default: `False`
    choose to copy the input data to new memory

subok : `bool`, optional, default: `True`
    allow passing of sub-classes by the array generator

Returns
-------
array : `Array`
    a new array, with a view of the data, and all associated metadata

methods.name: gwpy.types.index.Index.define
documentation:
Define a new `Index`.

The output is basically:

```
start + numpy.arange(num) * step
```

# Parameters

start

: The starting value of the index.

step

: The step size of the index.

num

: The size of the index (number of samples).

dtype

: The desired dtype of the index, if not given, defaults
  to the higher-precision dtype from `start` and `step`.

# Returns

index

: A new `Index` created from the given parameters.

modules.name: gwpy.types.array
documentation:
This module provides the `Array`.

The `Array` structure provides the core array-with-metadata environment
with the standard array methods wrapped to return instances of itself.

Each sub-class of `Array` should override the `Array._metadata_slots`
attribute, giving a list of the valid properties for these data. This is
critical to being able to view data with this class, used when copying and
transforming instances of the class.

methods.name: gwpy.types.array.Array.override_unit
documentation:
Forcefully reset the unit of these data

Use of this method is discouraged in favour of `to()`,
which performs accurate conversions from one unit to another.
The method should really only be used when the original unit of the
array is plain wrong.

Parameters
----------
unit : `~astropy.units.Unit`, `str`
    the unit to force onto this array
parse_strict : `str`, optional
    how to handle errors in the unit parsing, default is to
    raise the underlying exception from `astropy.units`

Raises
------
ValueError
    if a `str` cannot be parsed as a valid unit

methods.name: gwpy.types.array.Array.flatten
documentation:
Return a copy of the array collapsed into one dimension.

Any index information is removed as part of the flattening,
and the result is returned as a `~astropy.units.Quantity` array.

# Parameters

order

: 'C' means to flatten in row-major (C-style) order.
  'F' means to flatten in column-major (Fortran-
  style) order. 'A' means to flatten in column-major
  order if `a` is Fortran *contiguous* in memory,
  row-major order otherwise. 'K' means to flatten
  `a` in the order the elements occur in memory.
  The default is 'C'.

# Returns

y

: A copy of the input array, flattened to one dimension.

# See also

ravel : Return a flattened array.
flat : A 1-D flat iterator over the array.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> a = Array([[1,2], [3,4]], unit='m', name='Test')
>>> a.flatten()
<Quantity [1., 2., 3., 4.] m>
```

Array holding data with a unit, and other metadata

This `Array` holds the input data and a standard set of metadata
properties associated with GW data.

Parameters
----------
value : array-like
    input data array

unit : `~astropy.units.Unit`, optional
    physical unit of these data

epoch : `~gwpy.time.LIGOTimeGPS`, `float`, `str`, optional
    GPS epoch associated with these data,
    any input parsable by `~gwpy.time.to_gps` is fine

name : `str`, optional
    descriptive title for this array

channel : `~gwpy.detector.Channel`, `str`, optional
    source data stream for these data

dtype : `~numpy.dtype`, optional
    input data type

copy : `bool`, optional, default: `False`
    choose to copy the input data to new memory

subok : `bool`, optional, default: `True`
    allow passing of sub-classes by the array generator

Returns
-------
array : `Array`
    a new array, with a view of the data, and all associated metadata

Examples
--------
To create a new `Array` from a list of samples:

    >>> a = Array([1, 2, 3, 4, 5], 'm/s', name='my data')
    >>> print(a)
    Array([ 1., 2., 3., 4., 5.]
          unit: Unit("m / s"),
          name: 'my data',
          epoch: None,
          channel: None)

functions.name: gwpy.spectrogram.coherence._from_timeseries
documentation:
Generate a time-frequency coherence
{class}`~gwpy.spectrogram.Spectrogram` from a pair of
{class}`~gwpy.timeseries.TimeSeries`.

For each `stride`, a PSD {class}`~gwpy.frequencyseries.FrequencySeries`
is generated, with all resulting spectra stacked in time and returned.

functions.name: gwpy.spectrogram.coherence.from_timeseries
documentation:
Calculate the coherence `Spectrogram` between two `TimeSeries`.

# Parameters

timeseries

: input time-series to process.

stride

: number of seconds in single PSD (column of spectrogram).

fftlength

: number of seconds in single FFT.

overlap

: number of seconds of overlap between FFTs, defaults to no overlap

window

: window function to apply to timeseries prior to FFT.

nproc

: maximum number of independent frame reading processes, default
  is set to single-process file reading.

# Returns

spectrogram

: time-frequency power spectrogram as generated from the
  input time-series.

an index, whilst updating existing Index arrays as appropriate

>>> self._update_index("x", "dx", 0)
```

To actually set an index array, use `_set_index`

methods.name: gwpy.types.series.Series.step
documentation: Create a step plot of this series

methods.name: gwpy.types.series.Series.shift
documentation:
Shift this `Series` forward on the X-axis by `delta`

This modifies the series in-place.

# Parameters

delta

: The amount by which to shift (in x-axis units if `float`), give
  a negative value to shift backwards in time

# Examples

```pycon
>>> from gwpy.types import Series
>>> a = Series([1, 2, 3, 4, 5], x0=0, dx=1, xunit='m')
>>> print(a.x0)
0.0 m
>>> a.shift(5)
>>> print(a.x0)
5.0 m
>>> a.shift('-1 km')
-995.0 m
```

methods.name: gwpy.types.series.Series.value_at
documentation:
Return the value of this `Series` at the given `xindex` value

Parameters
----------
x : `float`, `~astropy.units.Quantity`
    the `xindex` value at which to search

Returns
-------
y : `~astropy.units.Quantity`
    the value of this Series at the given `xindex` value

methods.name: gwpy.types.series.Series.zip
documentation:
Zip the `xindex` and `value` arrays of this `Series`

# Returns

stacked

: The array formed by stacking the the `xindex` and `value` of this
  series

# Examples

```pycon
>>> a = Series([0, 2, 4, 6, 8], xindex=[-5, -4, -3, -2, -1])
>>> a.zip()
array([[-5.,  0.],
       [-4.,  2.],
       [-3.,  4.],
       [-2.,  6.],
       [-1.,  8.]])
```

methods.name: gwpy.types.series.Series.diff
documentation:
Calculate the n-th order discrete difference along given axis.

The first order difference is given by `out[n] = a[n+1] - a[n]` along
the given axis, higher order differences are calculated by using `diff`
recursively.

# Parameters

n

: The number of times values are differenced.

axis

: The axis along which the difference is taken, default is the
  last axis.

# Returns

diff

: The `n` order differences. The shape of the output is the same
  as the input, except along `axis` where the dimension is
  smaller by `n`.

# See also

numpy.diff

: for documentation on the underlying method

methods.name: gwpy.types.series.Series.is_contiguous
documentation:
Check whether other is contiguous with self.

Parameters
----------
other : `Series`, `numpy.ndarray`
    another series of the same type to test for contiguity

tol : `float`, optional
    the numerical tolerance of the test

Returns
-------
1
    if `other` is contiguous with this series, i.e. would attach
    seamlessly onto the end
-1
    if `other` is anti-contiguous with this seires, i.e. would attach
    seamlessly onto the start
0
    if `other` is completely dis-contiguous with thie series

Notes
-----
if a raw `numpy.ndarray` is passed as other, with no metadata, then
the contiguity check will always pass

methods.name: gwpy.types.series.Series.is_compatible
documentation:
Check whether this series and other have compatible metadata

This method tests that the `sample size <Series.dx>`, and the
`~Series.unit` match.

methods.name: gwpy.types.series.Series._compare_index
documentation:
Compare index attributes/arrays between self and other

# Raises

ValueError

: if `dx` doesn't match, or `xindex` values are not present/are
  identical (as appropriate)

methods.name: gwpy.types.series.Series._is_compatible_gwpy
documentation: Check whether this series and another series are compatible

methods.name: gwpy.types.series.Series._is_compatible_numpy
documentation: Check whether this series and a numpy.ndarray are compatible

methods.name: gwpy.types.series.Series.append
documentation:
Connect another series onto the end of the current one.

# Parameters

other

: another series of the same type to connect to this one

inplace

: perform operation in-place, modifying current series,
  otherwise copy data and return new series, default: `True`

  :::{warning}
  `inplace` append bypasses the reference check in
  `numpy.ndarray.resize`, so be carefully to only use this
  for arrays that haven't been sharing their memory!
  :::

pad

: value with which to pad discontiguous series,
  by default gaps will result in a `ValueError`.

gap

: action to perform if there's a gap between the other series
  and this one. One of

  - `'raise'` - raise a `ValueError`
  - `'ignore'` - remove gap and join data
  - `'pad'` - pad gap with zeros

  If `pad` is given and is not `None`, the default is `'pad'`,
  otherwise `'raise'`. If `gap='pad'` is given, the default
  for `pad` is `0`.

resize

: resize this array to accommodate new data, otherwise shift the
  old data to the left (potentially falling off the start) and
  put the new data in at the end, default: `True`.

# Returns

series

: a new series containing joined data sets

methods.name: gwpy.types.series.Series.prepend
documentation:
Connect another series onto the start of the current one.

# Parameters

other

: another series of the same type as this one

inplace

: perform operation in-place, modifying current series,
  otherwise copy data and return new series, default: `True`

  :::{warning}
  `inplace` prepend bypasses the reference check in
  `numpy.ndarray.resize`, so be carefully to only use this
  for arrays that haven't been sharing their memory!
  :::

pad

: value with which to pad discontiguous series,
  by default gaps will result in a `ValueError`.

gap

: action to perform if there's a gap between the other series
  and this one. One of

  - `'raise'` - raise a `ValueError`
  - `'ignore'` - remove gap and join data
  - `'pad'` - pad gap with zeros

  If `pad` is given and is not `None`, the default is `'pad'`,
  otherwise `'raise'`.

resize

: resize this array to accommodate new data, otherwise shift the
  old data to the left (potentially falling off the start) and
  put the new data in at the end, default: `True`.

# Returns

series

: time-series containing joined data sets

methods.name: gwpy.types.series.Series.update
documentation:
Update this series by appending new data from an other
and dropping the same amount of data off the start.

This is a convenience method that just calls `~Series.append` with
`resize=False`.

methods.name: gwpy.types.series.Series.crop
documentation:
Crop this series to the given x-axis extent.

# Parameters

start

: Lower limit of x-axis to crop to, defaults to
  {attr}`~Series.x0`.

end

: Upper limit of x-axis to crop to, defaults to series end.

copy

: Copy the input data to fresh memory,
  otherwise return a view (default).

# Returns

series

: A new series with a sub-set of the input data.

# Notes

If either `start` or `end` are outside of the original
`Series` span, warnings will be printed and the limits will
be restricted to the {attr}`~Series.xspan`.

methods.name: gwpy.types.series.Series.pad
documentation:
Pad this series to a new size

# Parameters

pad_width

: number of samples by which to pad each end of the array;
  given a single `int` to pad both ends by the same amount,
  or a (before, after) `tuple` for assymetric padding

kwargs

: see {meth}`numpy.pad` for kwarg documentation

# Returns

series

: the padded version of the input

# See also

numpy.pad

: for details on the underlying functionality

methods.name: gwpy.types.series.Series.inject
documentation:
Add two compatible `Series` along their shared x-axis values.

# Parameters

other

: a `Series` whose xindex intersects with `self.xindex`

# Returns

out

: the sum of `self` and `other` along their shared x-axis values

# Raises

ValueError

: if `self` and `other` have incompatible units or xindex intervals

# Notes

If `other.xindex` and `self.xindex` do not intersect, this method will
return a copy of `self`. If the series have uniformly offset indices,
this method will raise a warning.

If `self.xindex` is an array of timestamps, and if `other.xspan` is
not a subset of `self.xspan`, then `other` will be cropped before
being adding to `self`.

Users who wish to taper or window their `Series` should do so before
passing it to this method. See {meth}`TimeSeries.taper` and
{func}`~gwpy.signal.window.planck` for more information.

A one-dimensional data series

A `Series` is defined as an array of data indexed upon an axis, meaning
each sample maps to a position upon the axis. By convention the X axis
is used to define the index, with the `~Series.x0`, `~Series.dx`, and
`~Series.xindex` attributes allowing the positions of the data to be
well defined.

# Parameters

value

: input data array

unit

: physical unit of these data

x0

: the starting value for the x-axis of this array

dx

: the step size for the x-axis of this array

xindex

: the complete array of x-axis values for this array. This argument
  takes precedence over `x0` and `dx` so should be
  given in place of these if relevant, not alongside

xunit

: the unit of the x-axis index. If not given explicitly, it will be
  taken from any of `dx`, `x0`, or `xindex`, or set to a boring default

epoch

: GPS epoch associated with these data,
  any input parsable by `~gwpy.time.to_gps` is fine

name

: descriptive title for this array

channel

: source data stream for these data

dtype

: input data type

copy

: choose to copy the input data to new memory

subok

: allow passing of sub-classes by the array generator

# Returns

series

: a new `Series`

# Examples

To define a `Series` of displacements at a given input laser power,
for example:

```pycon
>>> data = Series([1, 2, 3, 2, 4, 3], unit='nm', x0=0, dx=2, xunit='W')
>>> print(data)
Series([ 1., 2., 3., 2., 4., 3.]
       unit: Unit("nm"),
       name: None,
       epoch: None,
       channel: None,
       x0: 0.0 W,
       dx: 2.0 W,
       xindex: [  0.   2.   4.   6.   8.  10.] W)
```

Build a new `Spectrogram` from a list of spectra.

Returns

methods.name: gwpy.spectrogram.spectrogram.Spectrogram.zpk
documentation:
Filter this `Spectrogram` by applying a zero-pole-gain filter

# Parameters

zeros

: list of zero frequencies (in Hertz)

poles

: list of pole frequencies (in Hertz)

gain

: DC gain of filter

analog

: type of ZPK being applied, if `analog=True` all parameters
  will be converted in the Z-domain for digital filtering

# Returns

specgram

: the frequency-domain filtered version of the input data

# See also

Spectrogram.filter

: for details on how a digital ZPK-format filter is applied

# Examples

To apply a zpk filter with file poles at 100 Hz, and five zeros at
1 Hz (giving an overall DC gain of 1e-10):

```
>>> data2 = data.zpk([100]*5, [1]*5, 1e-10)
```

methods.name: gwpy.spectrogram.spectrogram.Spectrogram.filter
documentation:
Apply the given filter to this `Spectrogram`.

# Parameters

filt

: 1, 2, 3, or 4 arguments defining the filter to be applied,

  > - an `Nx1` `~numpy.ndarray` of FIR coefficients
  > - an `Nx6` `~numpy.ndarray` of SOS coefficients
  > - `(numerator, denominator)` polynomials
  > - `(zeros, poles, gain)`
  > - `(A, B, C, D)` 'state-space' representation

analog

: if `True`, filter definition will be converted from Hertz
  to Z-domain digital representation, default: `False`

inplace

: if `True`, this array will be overwritten with the filtered
  version, default: `False`

# Returns

result

: the filtered version of the input `Spectrogram`,
  if `inplace=True` was given, this is just a reference to
  the modified input array

# Raises

ValueError

: if `filt` arguments cannot be interpreted properly

methods.name: gwpy.spectrogram.spectrogram.Spectrogram.variance
documentation:
Calculate the `SpectralVariance` of this `Spectrogram`.

# Parameters

bins

: array of histogram bin edges, including the rightmost edge

low

: left edge of lowest amplitude bin, only read
  if `bins` is not given

high

: right edge of highest amplitude bin, only read
  if `bins` is not given

nbins

: number of bins to generate, only read if `bins` is not
  given

log

: calculate amplitude bins over a logarithmic scale, only
  read if `bins` is not given

norm

: normalise bin counts to a unit sum

density

: normalise bin counts to a unit integral

# Returns

specvar

: 2D-array of spectral frequency-amplitude counts

# See also

numpy.histogram

: for details on specifying bins and weights

methods.name: gwpy.spectrogram.spectrogram.Spectrogram._wrap_function
documentation: -- Spectrogram ufuncs ---------------------

A 2D array holding a spectrogram of time-frequency data

# Parameters

value

: input data array

unit

: physical unit of these data

epoch

: GPS epoch associated with these data,
  any input parsable by `~gwpy.time.to_gps` is fine

sample_rate

: the rate of samples per second (Hertz)

times

: the complete array of GPS times accompanying the data for this series.
  This argument takes precedence over `epoch` and `sample_rate` so should
  be given in place of these if relevant, not alongside

f0

: starting frequency for these data

df

: frequency resolution for these data

frequencies

: the complete array of frequencies indexing the data.
  This argument takes precedence over `f0` and `df` so should
  be given in place of these if relevant, not alongside

epoch

: GPS epoch associated with these data,
  any input parsable by `~gwpy.time.to_gps` is fine

name

: descriptive title for this array

channel

: source data stream for these data

dtype

: input data type

copy

: choose to copy the input data to new memory

subok

: allow passing of sub-classes by the array generator

# Notes

Key methods:

```
.. autosummary::

   ~Spectrogram.read
   ~Spectrogram.write
   ~Spectrogram.plot
   ~Spectrogram.zpk
```

functions.name: gwpy.time._tconvert.tconvert
documentation:
Convert GPS times to ISO-format date-times and vice-versa.

# Parameters

gpsordate

: input gps or date to convert, many input types are supported

# Returns

date

: converted gps or date

# Notes

If the input object is a `float` or `LIGOTimeGPS`, it will get
converted from GPS format into a `datetime.datetime`, otherwise
the input will be converted into `LIGOTimeGPS`.

:::{warning}
This method cannot convert exact leap seconds to
`datetime.datetime`, that object doesn't support it,
so you should consider using `astropy.time.Time` directly.
:::

# Examples

Integers and floats are automatically converted from GPS to
`datetime.datetime`:

```pycon
>>> from gwpy.time import tconvert
>>> tconvert(0)
datetime.datetime(1980, 1, 6, 0, 0)
>>> tconvert(1126259462.3910)
datetime.datetime(2015, 9, 14, 9, 50, 45, 391000)
```

while strings are automatically converted to `~gwpy.time.LIGOTimeGPS`:

```pycon
>>> to_gps('Sep 14 2015 09:50:45.391')
LIGOTimeGPS(1126259462, 391000000)
```

Additionally, a few special-case words as supported, which all return
`~gwpy.time.LIGOTimeGPS`:

```pycon
>>> tconvert('now')
>>> tconvert('today')
>>> tconvert('tomorrow')
>>> tconvert('yesterday')
```

functions.name: gwpy.time._tconvert.to_gps
documentation:
Convert any input date/time into a `LIGOTimeGPS`.

Any input object that can be cast as a `~astropy.time.Time`
(with `str` going through the `datetime.datetime`) are acceptable.

# Parameters

t

: the input time, any object that can be converted into a
  `LIGOTimeGPS`, `~astropy.time.Time`, or `~datetime.datetime`,
  is acceptable.

args, kwargs

: other arguments to pass to pass to `~astropy.time.Time` if given

# Returns

gps

: the number of GPS seconds (non-integer) since the start of the
  epoch (January 6 1980).

# Raises

TypeError

: if a `str` input cannot be parsed as a `datetime.datetime`.

ValueError

: if the input cannot be cast as a `~astropy.time.Time` or
  `LIGOTimeGPS`.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> to_gps('Jan 1 2017')
LIGOTimeGPS(1167264018, 0)
>>> to_gps('Sep 14 2015 09:50:45.391')
LIGOTimeGPS(1126259462, 391000000)
```

```pycon
>>> import datetime
>>> to_gps(datetime.datetime(2017, 1, 1))
LIGOTimeGPS(1167264018, 0)
```

```pycon
>>> from astropy.time import Time
>>> to_gps(Time(57754, format='mjd'))
LIGOTimeGPS(1167264018, 0)
```

functions.name: gwpy.time._tconvert.from_gps
documentation:
Convert a GPS time into a `datetime.datetime`.

# Parameters

gps

: GPS time to convert

# Returns

datetime

: ISO-format datetime equivalent of input GPS time

# Notes

:::{warning}
This method cannot convert exact leap seconds to
`datetime.datetime`, that object doesn't support it,
so you should consider using `astropy.time.Time` directly.
:::

# Examples

```pycon
>>> from_gps(1167264018)
datetime.datetime(2017, 1, 1, 0, 0)
>>> from_gps(1126259462.3910)
datetime.datetime(2015, 9, 14, 9, 50, 45, 391000)
```

functions.name: gwpy.time._tconvert._time_to_gps
documentation:
Convert a `Time` into `LIGOTimeGPS`.

This method uses `datetime.datetime` underneath, which restricts
to microsecond precision by design. This should probably be fixed...

Parameters

time : `~astropy.time.Time`

Returns
-------
gps : `LIGOTimeGPS`
    Nano-second precision `LIGOTimeGPS` time

documentation:
Read a dict of series from one or more GWF files

# Parameters

source

: Source of data, any of the following:

  - `str` path of single data file,
  - `str` path of cache file,
  - `list` of paths.

channels

: a list of channels to read from the source.

start

: GPS start time of required data, anything parseable by
  {func}`~gwpy.time.to_gps` is fine.

end

: GPS end time of required data, anything parseable by
  {func}`~gwpy.time.to_gps` is fine.

scaled

: apply slope and bias calibration to ADC data.

type

: a `dict` of `(name, channel-type)` pairs, where `channel-type`
  can be one of `'adc'`, `'proc'`, or `'sim'`.

series_class

: the `Series` sub-type to return.

# Returns

data

: a dict of `(channel, series)` pairs read from the GWF source(s).

series_class

: the `Series` sub-type to return.

# Returns

data

: a dict of `(channel, series)` pairs read from the GWF file.

Read a series from an `FrData` structure

frdata

: the data structure to read

epoch

: the GPS start time of the containing frame
  (`LDAStools.frameCPP.FrameH.GTime`)

: the GPS start time of the user request

end

: the GPS end time of the user request

scaled

: apply slope and bias calibration to ADC data.

: the `Series` sub-type to return.

series

  `[start, end)` interval.

Read an array from an `FrVect` structure

vect

: the frame vector structur to read

start

: the GPS start time of the request

end

: the GPS end time of the request

: the GPS start time of the containing `FrData` structure

name

: the name of the output `series_class`; this is also used
  to ignore `FrVect` structures containing other information

series_class

: the `Series` sub-type to return.

functions.name: gwpy.timeseries.io.gwf.framecpp.write
documentation:
Write data to a GWF file using the frameCPP API

# Parameters

tsdict

: dict of data to write

outfile

: the file name of the target output file

start

: the GPS start time of the file

end

: the GPS end time of the file

type

: the type of the channel, one of 'adc', 'proc', 'sim', default
  is 'proc' unless stored in the channel structure

name

: the name of each frame

run

: the FrameH run number

compression

: name of compresion algorithm to use, or its endian-appropriate
  ID, choose from

  - `'RAW'`
  - `'GZIP'`
  - `'DIFF_GZIP'`
  - `'ZERO_SUPPRESS_WORD_2'`
  - `'ZERO_SUPPRESS_WORD_4'`
  - `'ZERO_SUPPRESS_WORD_8'`
  - `'ZERO_SUPPRESS_OTHERWISE_GZIP'`

compression_level

: compression level for given method, default is `6` for GZIP-based
  methods, otherwise `0`

modules.name: gwpy.timeseries.io.gwf
documentation:
Input/output routines for gravitational-wave frame (GWF) format files.

The frame format is defined in LIGO-T970130 available from dcc.ligo.org.

Currently supported are two separate libraries:

- `lalframe` : using the LIGO Algorithm Library Frame API (based off the
  FrameL library)
- `framecpp` : using the alternative `frameCPP` library

Due to the lower-level nature of the frameCPP python package, it is
preferred, in the instance that both lalframe and frameCPP are available
on a system.

functions.name: gwpy.timeseries.io.gwf.get_default_gwf_api
documentation:
Return the preferred GWF library

# Examples

If you have [LDAStools.frameCPP] installed:

```pycon
>>> from gwpy.timeseries.io.gwf import get_default_gwf_api
>>> get_default_gwf_api()
'framecpp'
```

Or, if you don't have [lalframe]:

```pycon
>>> get_default_gwf_api()
'lalframe'
```

Otherwise:

```pycon
>>> get_default_gwf_api()
ImportError: no GWF API available, please install a third-party GWF
library (framecpp, lalframe) and try again
```

functions.name: gwpy.timeseries.io.gwf.register_gwf_api
documentation:
Register a full set of GWF I/O methods for the given library

The given frame library must define the following methods

- `read`
  : to be contiguous, and should return a `TimeSeriesDict`
- `write`
  : and does all of the work

Additionally, the library must store the name of the third-party
dependency using the `FRAME_LIBRARY` variable.

functions.name: gwpy.timeseries.io.core._pad_series
documentation:
Pad a timeseries to match the specified [start, end) limits

To cover a gap in data returned from a data source.

Parameters
----------
ts : `gwpy.types.Series`
    the input series

pad : `float`, `astropy.units.Quantity`
    the value with which to pad

start : `float`, `astropy.units.Quantity`, optional
    the desired start point of the X-axis, defaults to
    the start point of the incoming series

end : `float`, `astropy.units.Quantity`, optional
    the desired end point of the X-axis, defaults to
    the end point of the incoming series

error : `bool`, optional
    raise `ValueError` when gaps are present, rather than padding
    anything

Returns
-------
series : instance of incoming series type
    a padded version of the series. This may be the same
    object if not padding is needed.

Raises
------
ValueError
    if `error=True` is given and padding would have been required
    to match the request.

functions.name: gwpy.timeseries.io.cache.preformat_cache
documentation:
Preprocess a `list` of file paths for reading.

This function does the following:

- read the list of paths cache file (if necessary),
- sort the cache in time order (if possible),
- sieve the cache to only include data we need.

Parameters
----------
cache : `list`, `str`, `pathlib.Path`
    List of file paths, or path to a cache file.

start : `~gwpy.time.LIGOTimeGPS`, `float`, `str`, optional
    GPS start time of required data, defaults to start of data found;
    any input parseable by `~gwpy.time.to_gps` is fine.

end : `~gwpy.time.LIGOTimeGPS`, `float`, `str`, optional
    GPS end time of required data, defaults to end of data found;
    any input parseable by `~gwpy.time.to_gps` is fine.

sort : `callable`, optional
    A callable key function by which to sort the file paths.

Returns
-------
modcache : `list`
    A parsed, sieved list of paths based on the input arguments.

See also
--------
gwpy.io.cache.read_cache
    For details of how the sorting and sieving is implemented

modules.name: gwpy.timeseries.statevector
documentation:
This module defines the Boolean array representing the state of some data

Such states are typically the comparison of a `TimeSeries` against some
threshold, where sub-threshold is good and sup-threshold is bad,
for example.

Single `StateTimeSeries` can be bundled together to form `StateVector`
arrays, representing a bit mask of states that combine to make a detailed
statement of instrumental operation
-*- coding: utf-8 -*-

Cardiff University (2017-2021)
This file is part of GWpy.

MERCHANTABILITY or FITNESS FOR A PARTICULAR PURPOSE.  See the

You should have received a copy of the GNU General Public License
along with GWpy.  If not, see <http://www.gnu.org/licenses/>.

functions.name: gwpy.timeseries.statevector._bool_segments
documentation:
Yield segments of consecutive `True` values in a boolean array

# Parameters

array

: An iterable of boolean-castable values.

start

: The value of the first sample on the indexed axis
  (e.g.the GPS start time of the array).

delta

: The step size on the indexed axis (e.g. sample duration).

minlen

: The minimum number of consecutive `True` values for a segment.

# Yields

segment

: `(start + i * delta, start + (i + n) * delta)` for a sequence
  of `n` consecutive True values starting at position `i`.

# Notes

This method is adapted from original code written by Kipp Cannon and
distributed under GPLv3.

The datatype of the values returned will be the larger of the types
of `start` and `delta`.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> print(list(_bool_segments([0, 1, 0, 0, 0, 1, 1, 1, 0, 1]))
[(1, 2), (5, 8), (9, 10)]
>>> print(list(_bool_segments([0, 1, 0, 0, 0, 1, 1, 1, 0, 1]
...                           start=100., delta=0.1))
[(100.1, 100.2), (100.5, 100.8), (100.9, 101.0)]
```

methods.name: gwpy.timeseries.statevector.StateTimeSeries.to_dqflag
documentation:
Convert this series into a `~gwpy.segments.DataQualityFlag`.

Each contiguous set of `True` values are grouped as a
`~gwpy.segments.Segment` running from the GPS time the first
found `True`, to the GPS time of the next `False` (or the end
of the series)

# Parameters

minlen

: minimum number of consecutive `True` values to identify as a
  `~gwpy.segments.Segment`. This is useful to ignore single
  bit flips, for example.

dtype

: output segment entry type, can pass either a type for simple
  casting, or a callable function that accepts a float and returns
  another numeric type, defaults to the `dtype` of the time index

round

: choose to round each `~gwpy.segments.Segment` to its
  inclusive integer boundaries

label

: the {attr}`~gwpy.segments.DataQualityFlag.label` for the
  output flag.

description

: the {attr}`~gwpy.segments.DataQualityFlag.description` for the
  output flag.

# Returns

dqflag

: a segment representation of this `StateTimeSeries`, the span
  defines the `known` segments, while the contiguous `True`
  sets defined each of the `active` segments

methods.name: gwpy.timeseries.statevector.StateTimeSeries.to_lal
documentation: Bogus function inherited from superclass, do not use.

Boolean array representing a good/bad state determination

# Parameters

value

: input data array

t0

: GPS epoch associated with these data,
  any input parsable by `~gwpy.time.to_gps` is fine

dt

: time between successive samples (seconds), can also be given inversely
  via `sample_rate`

sample_rate

: the rate of samples per second (Hertz), can also be given inversely
  via `dt`

times

: the complete array of GPS times accompanying the data for this series.
  This argument takes precedence over `t0` and `dt` so should be given
  in place of these if relevant, not alongside

name

: descriptive title for this array

channel

: source data stream for these data

dtype

: input data type

copy

: choose to copy the input data to new memory

subok

: allow passing of sub-classes by the array generator

# Notes

Key methods

```
.. autosummary::

   ~StateTimeSeries.to_dqflag
```
